title: Blood on Wool
stainChemistry: Blood and wool share a chemistry problem: both are protein. Wool is keratin — a lock together protein fiber itself — and the enzymatic digestants that safely dissolve blood on cotton ALSO dissolve wool. That rules out nearly every standard protein-stain tool. Wool is also heat-sensitive, alkali-sensitive, and chlorine-bleach-intolerant. The workable window is narrow: cool water (≤30°C / 86°F), gentle detergent, minimal agitation, maybe a short peroxide spot if the shadow is tannic-brown (old). Wet agitation felts wool permanently — the mechanical action, not the chemistry, is often what ruins a wool garment.
whyThisWorks: Cold water halts protein setting without cooking wool keratin. Mild NSD or a wool-rated detergent lifts the soluble protein without attacking the fiber. 3% hydrogen peroxide in small quantity oxidizes residual iron-based brown pigment — safe on wool at short dwell times, unlike chlorine bleach. The critical rule is NO ENZYMES and NO WARM WATER — both destroy wool directly. Agitation is also a failure mode: rubbing, wringing, or machine action felts the fiber.
defaultAssumption: Wool is delicate enough that the home path routes to a specialist if the stain is anything beyond fresh. Pro spotters can do more, but carefully.

spottingProtocol.technique: Blot from the back, no rubbing
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply cool water (≤30°C / 86°F) to the back of the stain with a spray bottle or damp cloth. Blot from underneath — lift the stain out rather than working it sideways through the fibers. Flip and blot from the front. Temperatures above 30°C shrink wool keratin; mechanical agitation (rubbing, wringing) felts the fiber permanently.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Neutral Synthetic Detergent or wool-rated detergent (Woolite, Eucalan)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply a tiny amount of mild NSD or a wool-rated detergent directly to the stain. Work it in very gently with a fingertip — no brush, no scrubbing. NEVER use enzyme pretreats on wool — protein-digesting enzymes damage wool as readily as it digests blood. This is the single most common way home attempts destroy a wool garment.

Rinse with cool water, from the back of the fabric. Do NOT wring, twist, or rub — press the water out between layers of towel instead. Twisting wet wool felts the fiber; pressed drying preserves the hand.

For residual brown shadow on aged blood: test a drop of 3% hydrogen peroxide on an inside seam first. If the wool holds color: dab peroxide on a cotton swab, touch the shadow only, watch for 30–60 seconds, rinse cool. Never saturate wool with peroxide — short, targeted contact only.

spottingProtocol.technique: No heat, no hang, no sun
spottingProtocol.instruction: Press the garment between dry towels to absorb moisture — do not wring. Lay flat on a fresh dry towel to air-dry at room temperature. Never hang wet wool (stretches out of shape), never dry in direct sun (fades dye + weakens fiber), never use heat (felts and shrinks). Slow, flat, cool drying preserves the fiber.

steps: 1. Cool water (≤30°C) from the back, blot only — no rubbing, no wringing.
2. Mild NSD or wool-rated detergent, fingertip application. NEVER enzymes.
3. Cool flush from the back, press dry between towels (do not wring).
4. For aged shadow: 3% H₂O₂ on a cotton swab after inside-seam test. Short contact only.
5. Press dry, lay flat, air-dry at room temperature. No heat, no hang, no sun.
products: Wool-rated consumer detergent (Woolite, Eucalan) as substitute
Hydrogen Peroxide 3% for aged stains only — cotton swab, spot-only application
professionalProtocol.temperature: ≤30°C / 86°F for any wet step; room temperature drying
warnings: NEVER use enzyme pretreats, protein spotters, or protein-digesting enzyme-based digestants on wool — they digest keratin
Never use hot water, warm water, or heat drying — shrinks and felts wool
Never use chlorine bleach on wool — destroys the fiber
Never rub, wring, or twist wet wool — felting is mechanical, permanent, and irreversible
Never hang wet wool — stretches out of shape

homeSolutions.instruction: Before anything else, understand this: the enzyme pretreats (OxiClean, Biz, Zout, any "enzyme" stain remover) that work on cotton-blood will DESTROY wool. Wool is protein, and protein-digesting enzymes digest it. If you have enzyme stain remover and a wool garment, they don't go together.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Enzymes digest wool keratin. The most common way home attempts ruin wool is with an enzyme pretreat.

homeSolutions.instruction: The rule for wool is cooler than cool. Room temperature or under. Hot water shrinks wool permanently in under a minute. Even warm water starts the felting process. Use cold to very cool water throughout.

homeSolutions.agent: Blot from the Back — No Rubbing
homeSolutions.instruction: Rubbing wool when wet felts the fiber — turns a soft wool garment into a shrunken, stiff version of itself. Instead: lay the garment stain-side DOWN on a folded white towel. Spray or dab cold water onto the back of the stain. Let the blood transfer downward into the towel underneath. Swap the towel for a fresh section, repeat.

Use a wool-specific detergent — Woolite, Eucalan, or any detergent labeled "safe for wool." Put a tiny amount on a fingertip and work it into the stain VERY gently — no brush, no scrubbing. Dawn can work in a pinch but wool-rated is better. Absolutely no enzyme or "oxi" products.

Rinse with cool water, gentle stream, from the back of the fabric. Do NOT wring — wringing felts wool. Instead, press the garment between two dry towels to absorb water. Roll it up between the towels like a burrito and press — do not twist.

homeSolutions.agent: Peroxide Only If Shadow Is Old
homeSolutions.instruction: If a faint brown shadow remains after a fresh stain, you can try 3% hydrogen peroxide on a cotton swab — but test on an inside seam first. Wool can handle a quick peroxide touch, but any wet soaking or prolonged contact weakens the fiber and fades the dye. Dab only, 30 seconds, rinse cool.

homeSolutions.agent: Lay Flat to Dry — No Hanger, No Sun
homeSolutions.instruction: Put the garment on a fresh dry towel on a flat surface (table, drying rack on flat). Never hang wet wool — the weight of water stretches it out of shape permanently. Never dry in direct sun — UV degrades the fiber and fades the dye. Air-dry at room temperature, often overnight. Patience here is the whole job.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate — Wool Specialists Are Worth It
homeSolutions.instruction: Wool garments are expensive and fragile. If the blood is older than a day, was already heat-set, or you're unsure what you're dealing with — take it to a specialist dry cleaner with real wool experience (ask; not every cleaner has this). Tell them: "Blood on wool, age [time], treated at home with cool water + wool-rated detergent, no enzymes, no heat, no wringing."

steps: 1. No enzymes. No warm water. No rubbing. No wringing. Those are the four permanent-damage rules.
2. Cool water (room temp or below), sprayed onto the back of the stain while the garment lies stain-down on a dry towel.
3. Wool-rated detergent (Woolite, Eucalan) on a fingertip, worked in very gently.
4. Cool rinse from the back. Press out water between two dry towels — do not wring.
5. For aged shadow only: 3% hydrogen peroxide on a cotton swab after inside-seam test.
6. Lay flat on a dry towel to air-dry. Never hang. Never sun. Never heat.
7. Older or unclear stains: call a wool-experienced dry cleaner.
products: 3% hydrogen peroxide + cotton swabs (for aged shadow only)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the blood is older than 24 hours, if any enzymes or heat were already applied, or if the garment is a prized wool item. Call a wool-experienced dry cleaner.
warnings: NEVER use enzyme pretreats or 'oxi' products — they digest wool
NEVER use hot or warm water — shrinks wool permanently
NEVER rub, wring, or twist wet wool — felts the fiber
NEVER hang wet wool — stretches out of shape permanently
NEVER use chlorine bleach — destroys wool

neverDo: NEVER use enzymes / protein-digesting enzyme / 'oxi' on wool — digests keratin
NEVER use hot or warm water — shrinks and felts
NEVER rub, wring, or twist wet wool — mechanical felting is permanent
NEVER use chlorine bleach — destroys wool
NEVER hang wet wool — stretches out of shape
fiberSensitivities: Wool is protein (keratin) — any protein-digesting enzyme-based cleaner digests it
Wet agitation causes felting regardless of temperature
Wool is alkali-sensitive (no high-pH detergents) and chlorine-intolerant

materialWarnings: No enzymes or oxi products — digest wool keratin
No warm water, no heat drying
No rubbing or wringing — felts the fiber

escalation.when: Blood older than 24 hours, any prior enzyme or heat exposure, prized wool garment (cashmere, merino, suit fabric), or two gentle home passes without clearing it.
escalation.whatToTell: "Blood on wool, age [time], treated at home with cool water + wool-rated detergent, no enzymes, no heat, no wringing." Ask for a cleaner with real wool experience — not every dry cleaner has it.

contextQuestions: Has any enzyme product, hot water, or heat already been applied?
What kind of wool — merino, cashmere, suit fabric, carpet, blanket?
Has the garment been rubbed, twisted, or wrung when wet?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Any prior enzyme exposure is usually irreversible — wool keratin is gone. Prior heat or agitation causes permanent shrinkage / felting. Fresh + no heat + gentle = highest home success rate, but always route nice wool to a pro.
